title: Determination of the Fermi Energy of Diamond using Photoluminescence Spectral Analysis
authors: Yifan Song, Sina Ilkhani, Leah Webb, Helen Highland, Shunki Nakamura, Stephen B. Cronin, Susumu Takahashi
abstract: Electronic band structures and the Fermi energy provide essential information for understanding the electronic properties of solids. In semiconductors, the Fermi energy level is determined by the donor and acceptor concentrations. For diamond, the relationship between the Fermi energy level and the donor-acceptor concentrations is highly nonlinear; therefore, experimental determination of the Fermi energy level is important. Here, we report a method to determine the Fermi energy of diamond based on photoluminescence (PL) measurement. The density-functional-theory (DFT) study by Deák et al.~\cite{deak2014formation} showed the relationship between the Fermi energy and the formation energies of nitrogen-vacancy centers in the negatively charged (NV-) and neutrally charged (NV0) charge states. In the present method, we measure the relative populations of the NV- and NV0 centers from PL spectral analysis and, using these populations and the DFT result, determine the Fermi energy of the diamond samples. Moreover, we show the application of the method to study the spin coherence and the stability against the charge state conversion of the NV centers on several diamond samples. We also extend the method for the Fermi energy determination using the silicon-vacancy (SiV) center in diamond. The PL-based method is advantageous for determining the Fermi energy with high spatial and fast time resolutions, even in extreme environments, and can be extended to determine various wide band gap semiconductors.
summary: This paper presents a new experimental method to precisely determine the Fermi energy in diamond using photoluminescence spectroscopy. By measuring the ratio of different charge states of NV and SiV centers and applying DFT-based models, the researchers can map the Fermi energy across different diamond types. The study also demonstrates how this energy level affects spin coherence times and charge state stability. This technique is highly advantageous because it offers high spatial and temporal resolution and can function in extreme environments.
main_problem: The difficulty of experimentally determining the Fermi energy in diamond due to the highly non-linear relationship between Fermi energy and donor-acceptor concentrations.
main_result: The development of a non-destructive method to determine the Fermi energy by analyzing the relative populations of NV and SiV charge states via photoluminescence spectroscopy.
method: The method combines photoluminescence (PL) spectral decomposition with Density Functional Theory (DFT) models to relate charge state populations to the Fermi energy.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of various diamond crystals (CVD, HPHT, and polycrystalline) containing nitrogen-vacancy (NV) and silicon-vacancy (SiV) defect centers.
key_observables: Relative populations of NV- and NV0 centers, spin coherence time (T2), and PL intensity.
important_parameters: Fermi energy (Ef), nitrogen donor concentration (P1 centers), and laser power dependence.
main_assumptions: The method relies on the accuracy of DFT-derived formation energies and assumes the ability to extrapolate populations to zero laser power to avoid laser-induced charge state conversion.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows energy diagrams and DFT-calculated formation energies; Figure 2 displays PL spectra and the results of spectral decomposition analysis; Figure 3 shows the dependence of parameters on specific variables.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of Fermi energy determination, presents the proposed PL-based method using DFT integration, demonstrates the method on various nitrogen-doped and thermal-grade diamond samples, investigates the correlation between Fermi energy and spin coherence (T2), extends the method to SiV centers, and concludes with the method's advantages and potential applications.
why_interesting: This is relevant for quantum information because controlling the charge state stability and spin coherence of NV centers is critical for the development of robust solid-state qubits and quantum sensors.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. NV charge states and the Fermi energy. (a) The energy diagram for different NV charge states with respect to the conduction band and valence band. (b) The DFT calculation of the formation energy of the NV center as a function of Fermi energy [1]. (c) The Relative populations of NV charge states as a function of Fermi energy.

FIG. 2. PL spectrum decomposition analysis. (a) PL spectrum of Sample 1. The PL intensity is normalized by the measurement time. The data was taken with a laser power of 7 mW and an integration time of 750 ms. The y-axis shows the normalized intensity in units of (count/s). From the fit, we obtained cNV −and cNV 0 were 1.46×106 and 2.95×106, giving [NV 0] and [NV −] to be 55.0 ± 0.2% and 45.0 ± 0.2 %, respectively. (b) PL spectrum of Sample 4. The data was taken with a laser power of 10 mW and an integration time of 5 s. The y-axis shows the normalized intensity in units of (count/s). cNV −and cNV 0 were 2059 and 1367, giving [NV 0] and [NV 0] to be 60.0 ± 0.2 and 40.0 ± 0.2 %,...

FIG. 3. [NV −] and [NV 0] as a function of laser power. (a)-(b) results from Sample 1 and 4, respectively. Black zand orange points: experimental data (square: NV0, dot: NV−); red line: fitting. The obtained fitting parameters were the following. (a) Sample 1. A1,NV 0 = 0.43 ± 0.03, A2,NV 0 = 0.32 ± 0.02, A1,NV −= 0.57 ± 0.03, A2,NV 0 = 0.68 ± 0.02, x0 = 0.8 ± 0.5, and n = 1 ± 1. (b) Sample 4. A1,NV 0 = 0.778 ± 0.001, A2,NV 0 = 0.601 ± 0.001, A1,NV −= 0.222 ± 0.001, A2,NV 0 = 0.399 ± 0.001, x0 = 3.90±0.04, and n = 1.40±0.02. For Samples 4, 5, and 6, we obtained n = 1.40±0.01, 0.6±0.6, and 1.5±0.6, respectively. For Samples 7 and 8, we found n = 1 ± 1 and 1 ± 3, respectively. (c) Relative...

FIG. 4. Dependence of the Fermi energy (EF ) on 1/T2. The inset shows T2 as a function of EF . A red dashed line represents the fit with Eq. 7. In the fit, β = 38.68 (eV−1), corresponding to T = 300 K,and x0 = 2.57 (eV) were used. from the fit result, we obtained A = 0.010 ± 0.004 (µs−1) and Γ = 0.002 ± 0.012 (µs−1). The obtained Γ value agrees with the contribution from the nuclear decoherence [27].
